subject: Chemistry
title: Seaborgium: Named While He Lived
description: A quick, practical guide to Seaborgium: Named While He Lived.

hook.text: Glenn Seaborg is the only person to have an element named after him while he was still alive.

buildup.text: Seaborg helped discover ten elements, including plutonium, curium, and californium. He won the 1951 Nobel Prize.

discovery.text: In 1974, element 106 was created. American scientists proposed naming it seaborgium in his honor.

twist.text: IUPAC initially refused—a long tradition said elements can't be named after living people. A naming war erupted.

climax.text: After years of debate, seaborgium was approved in 1997. Seaborg died two years later.

punchline.text: Ten elements discovered. One named after him. While he was alive.

quiz.question: Why was naming element 106 'seaborgium' controversial?
options: Tradition said elements shouldn't be named after living people
Seaborg had not contributed enough to chemistry
Another country wanted to name it differently
